title: Event gap detection with LAG and conditional revenue split
template:
WITH entity_dates AS (
    SELECT entity_id, DATE(event_timestamp) AS event_date, SUM(value) AS daily_value
    FROM events
    WHERE status NOT IN ('canceled')
    GROUP BY entity_id, DATE(event_timestamp)
),
gaps AS (
    SELECT entity_id, event_date, daily_value,
        LAG(event_date) OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY event_date) AS prev_date,
        DATEDIFF(event_date, LAG(event_date) OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY event_date)) AS gap_days
    FROM entity_dates
),
significant_gaps AS (
    SELECT entity_id,
        prev_date  AS gap_start,
        event_date AS gap_end,
        gap_days,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY gap_days DESC) AS gap_rank
    FROM gaps
    WHERE gap_days >= 60
),
top_gap AS (SELECT * FROM significant_gaps WHERE gap_rank = 1),
revenue_split AS (
    SELECT ed.entity_id,
        SUM(CASE WHEN ed.event_date <= tg.gap_start THEN ed.daily_value ELSE 0 END) AS value_before_gap,
        SUM(CASE WHEN ed.event_date >= tg.gap_end   THEN ed.daily_value ELSE 0 END) AS value_after_gap
    FROM entity_dates ed
    JOIN top_gap tg ON ed.entity_id = tg.entity_id
    GROUP BY ed.entity_id
)
SELECT tg.entity_id, tg.gap_days AS longest_gap_days, tg.gap_start, tg.gap_end,
    ROUND(rs.value_before_gap, 2) AS revenue_before_gap,
    ROUND(rs.value_after_gap,  2) AS revenue_after_gap
FROM top_gap tg
JOIN revenue_split rs ON tg.entity_id = rs.entity_id
ORDER BY tg.gap_days DESC
when_to_use: find entities (sellers, customers) that went inactive for N+ consecutive days then resumed
compute the longest gap between consecutive events per entity
split any metric (revenue, orders, logins) into 'before event X' and 'after event X' using conditional SUM(CASE WHEN date <= boundary THEN ...)
churn resurrection analysis, seller reactivation, customer win-back
anti_patterns: using GROUP BY date without LAG — you need the PREVIOUS date per entity to compute gap; LAG() OVER (PARTITION BY entity ORDER BY date) is mandatory
computing gaps at the order level instead of deduplicating to one row per (entity, date) first — multiple orders on the same day create false small gaps
using DATEDIFF on raw timestamps instead of DATE() — time-of-day differences inflate gap counts
picking the largest gap without ROW_NUMBER() — a single entity may have multiple 60+ day gaps; rank to find the worst one
conditional SUM for revenue split: use <= gap_start for 'before' and >= gap_end for 'after' — the boundary dates themselves belong to the period they closed/opened
notes: LAG(date) OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY date) gives the previous event date; DATEDIFF on (current, previous) is the gap.
To find the longest gap per entity: filter WHERE gap_days >= threshold, then ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY gap_days DESC), keep rank = 1.
Revenue split pattern: SUM(CASE WHEN event_date <= gap_start THEN value ELSE 0 END) — this is simpler and faster than joining two separate date-filtered subqueries.

title: Monthly cohort retention table (pivot via CASE WHEN)
template:
WITH customer_events AS (
    SELECT customer_unique_id,
        DATE_FORMAT(event_timestamp, '%Y-%m-01') AS event_month
    FROM customers c
    JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
    WHERE o.order_status NOT IN ('canceled')
    GROUP BY customer_unique_id, DATE_FORMAT(event_timestamp, '%Y-%m-01')
),
first_event AS (
    SELECT customer_unique_id, MIN(event_month) AS cohort_month
    FROM customer_events
    GROUP BY customer_unique_id
),
activity AS (
    SELECT fp.customer_unique_id, fp.cohort_month,
        TIMESTAMPDIFF(MONTH, fp.cohort_month, ce.event_month) AS months_since_first
    FROM first_event fp
    JOIN customer_events ce ON fp.customer_unique_id = ce.customer_unique_id
),
cohort_sizes AS (
    SELECT cohort_month, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_unique_id) AS cohort_size
    FROM first_event
    GROUP BY cohort_month
)
SELECT
    cs.cohort_month,
    cs.cohort_size,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN a.months_since_first = 1  THEN a.customer_unique_id END) / cs.cohort_size, 1) AS ret_m1,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN a.months_since_first = 2  THEN a.customer_unique_id END) / cs.cohort_size, 1) AS ret_m2,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN a.months_since_first = 3  THEN a.customer_unique_id END) / cs.cohort_size, 1) AS ret_m3,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN a.months_since_first = 6  THEN a.customer_unique_id END) / cs.cohort_size, 1) AS ret_m6,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN a.months_since_first = 12 THEN a.customer_unique_id END) / cs.cohort_size, 1) AS ret_m12
FROM cohort_sizes cs
JOIN activity a ON cs.cohort_month = a.cohort_month
GROUP BY cs.cohort_month, cs.cohort_size
ORDER BY cs.cohort_month
when_to_use: monthly cohort retention table showing % of customers who returned at M+1, M+2, M+3, M+6, M+12
cohort analysis anchored to first purchase/event month
any pivot of per-customer activity into fixed month-offset columns
subscription or e-commerce repeat purchase funnel
anti_patterns: using customer_id instead of customer_unique_id when a single real person can have multiple IDs (e.g. Olist creates a new customer_id per order) — always deduplicate to the true person identifier
counting rows instead of COUNT(DISTINCT customer_unique_id) for retention — one customer with 3 orders in month 2 must count as 1 retained customer, not 3
using DATEDIFF / 30 for month offset — month lengths vary; TIMESTAMPDIFF(MONTH, ...) handles this correctly
joining activity back to cohort_sizes without GROUP BY cohort_month in the final SELECT — produces fan-out and inflated cohort_size
including the cohort month itself (months_since_first = 0) in the retention columns — that is the acquisition row, not a return visit
notes: TIMESTAMPDIFF(MONTH, cohort_month, activity_month) returns the integer month offset; month +1 = returned next calendar month.
Cohort size = COUNT(DISTINCT customer_unique_id) in first_event, grouped by cohort_month.
The CASE WHEN pivot pattern works for any finite set of offsets; for arbitrary offsets use a separate activity_offsets table and avoid wide pivots.
For Exasol / Postgres use DATE_TRUNC('month', ts) instead of DATE_FORMAT.

title: Multi-condition anomaly detection (ratio + count + anti-join)
template:
WITH order_item_totals AS (
    SELECT order_id, ROUND(SUM(price + freight_value), 2) AS items_total
    FROM order_items
    GROUP BY order_id
),
order_payment_totals AS (
    SELECT order_id,
        ROUND(SUM(payment_value), 2)      AS payment_total,
        COUNT(DISTINCT payment_type)       AS distinct_payment_types
    FROM order_payments
    GROUP BY order_id
),
reviewed_customers AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT c.customer_unique_id
    FROM order_reviews r
    JOIN orders o      ON r.order_id   = o.order_id
    JOIN customers c   ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
)
SELECT o.order_id, c.customer_unique_id, c.customer_state,
    oit.items_total, opt.payment_total,
    ROUND(opt.payment_total - oit.items_total, 2) AS overpayment,
    opt.distinct_payment_types
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c              ON o.customer_id  = c.customer_id
JOIN order_item_totals oit    ON o.order_id      = oit.order_id
JOIN order_payment_totals opt ON o.order_id      = opt.order_id
WHERE opt.payment_total > oit.items_total * 1.01
  AND opt.distinct_payment_types > 1
  AND c.customer_unique_id NOT IN (SELECT customer_unique_id FROM reviewed_customers)
ORDER BY overpayment DESC
when_to_use: detect anomalous orders using multiple independent conditions: a ratio threshold, a count threshold, and an existence check
payment fraud: overpayment > 1% AND split payment types AND customer never reviewed
any pattern where you pre-aggregate from 2-3 tables into CTEs, then apply compound WHERE filters on the aggregated results
anti-join (NOT IN / NOT EXISTS) to exclude entities with a history in another table
anti_patterns: applying ratio filter before aggregation — payment_value must be SUMmed per order first; filtering individual payment rows gives wrong results
using customer_id instead of customer_unique_id for the 'never reviewed' check — one real customer with multiple customer_ids could be missed
using NOT IN with a subquery that can return NULLs — NOT IN returns no rows if the subquery contains any NULL; use NOT EXISTS or add WHERE customer_unique_id IS NOT NULL in the subquery
the 1% threshold (items_total * 1.01) is intentional to absorb floating-point rounding; using strict equality = fails on nearly-matching amounts
notes: Pattern: pre-aggregate each table independently in CTEs → join on order_id → apply compound WHERE filters. This keeps the main query clean and each CTE testable.
To avoid NOT IN / NULL trap: use NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM reviewed_customers rc WHERE rc.customer_unique_id = c.customer_unique_id) instead.

title: Composite weighted score from multiple LEFT JOIN paths + PERCENT_RANK
template:
WITH metric_a AS (
    SELECT entity_id, AVG(score_col) / 5.0 AS normalized_a
    FROM table_a
    JOIN related ON table_a.id = related.id
    GROUP BY entity_id
),
metric_b AS (
    SELECT entity_id,
        COUNT(*) AS total_b,
        SUM(CASE WHEN condition THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS passing_b
    FROM table_b
    WHERE status = 'completed'
    GROUP BY entity_id
),
metric_c AS (
    SELECT entity_id,
        COUNT(DISTINCT item_id) AS total_c,
        COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN item_count > 1 THEN item_id END) AS repeat_c
    FROM (SELECT entity_id, item_id, COUNT(*) AS item_count FROM detail_table GROUP BY 1, 2) sub
    GROUP BY entity_id
),
scored AS (
    SELECT e.entity_id,
        COALESCE(a.normalized_a, 0) * 0.4
        + COALESCE(b.passing_b * 1.0 / NULLIF(b.total_b, 0), 0) * 0.4
        + COALESCE(c.repeat_c  * 1.0 / NULLIF(c.total_c,  0), 0) * 0.2  AS composite_score
    FROM entities e
    LEFT JOIN metric_a a ON e.entity_id = a.entity_id
    LEFT JOIN metric_b b ON e.entity_id = b.entity_id
    LEFT JOIN metric_c c ON e.entity_id = c.entity_id
)
SELECT entity_id, composite_score,
    ROUND(PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY composite_score), 4) AS percentile_rank
FROM scored
ORDER BY composite_score DESC
when_to_use: build a composite KPI score from 3+ independently sourced metrics (each from different join paths)
seller/vendor/employee performance ranking with weighted components
normalize metrics to 0–1 scale before blending: rates are already 0–1; point scores divide by max (e.g. /5.0)
PERCENT_RANK() to assign global percentile position after scoring
anti_patterns: using INNER JOIN instead of LEFT JOIN for each metric CTE — sellers with no reviews or no deliveries will be dropped entirely; LEFT JOIN + COALESCE(value, 0) penalises them correctly
forgetting NULLIF in the denominator — division by zero when a seller has 0 delivered orders; always wrap: passing / NULLIF(total, 0)
summing metrics with different scales without normalising first — a 0–5 review score added directly to a 0–1 delivery rate distorts the blend
recomputing the same window function multiple times in one SELECT for tier assignment — compute PERCENT_RANK once in a subquery/CTE and reference it
notes: Pattern: one CTE per metric → all CTEs LEFT JOIN onto the master entity table → COALESCE each to 0 → blend with weights → window rank over blended score.
Weights must sum to 1.0. If a dimension is unavailable for an entity it scores 0 on that dimension (not excluded).
PERCENT_RANK() returns 0 for the lowest scorer, 1 for the highest (if ORDER BY DESC, 0 = best).

title: Dual non-overlapping rolling windows for period-over-period comparison and anomaly flagging
template:
WITH daily_total AS (
    SELECT order_date, SUM(revenue) AS day_revenue
    FROM daily_metrics
    GROUP BY order_date
),
rolling_windows AS (
    SELECT order_date, day_revenue,
        SUM(day_revenue) OVER (
            ORDER BY order_date
            ROWS BETWEEN 29 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
        ) AS rolling_current,
        SUM(day_revenue) OVER (
            ORDER BY order_date
            ROWS BETWEEN 59 PRECEDING AND 30 PRECEDING
        ) AS rolling_prior
    FROM daily_total
),
anomalies AS (
    SELECT order_date, rolling_current, rolling_prior,
        ROUND((rolling_current - rolling_prior) / NULLIF(rolling_prior, 0) * 100, 2) AS pct_change
    FROM rolling_windows
    WHERE rolling_prior IS NOT NULL
      AND rolling_current < rolling_prior * 0.80
)
SELECT * FROM anomalies ORDER BY order_date
when_to_use: compare a rolling N-day window to the immediately preceding N-day window (non-overlapping)
flag days where rolling revenue dropped more than X% vs the prior window
period-over-period change on a rolling basis rather than calendar month/week
find anomaly days in a time series and then drill into category/segment breakdown for that window
anti_patterns: using ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for both windows — they will overlap; the prior window must use ROWS BETWEEN 2N-1 PRECEDING AND N PRECEDING to be non-overlapping
using RANGE BETWEEN instead of ROWS BETWEEN on date columns — RANGE groups equal values; if two rows share a date both are included in the boundary, corrupting the window size
checking rolling_prior IS NOT NULL but forgetting the warm-up period — the first 30 rows have no valid prior window; the IS NOT NULL check handles this for ROWS BETWEEN (it returns NULL naturally)
applying the anomaly filter inside the window CTE — always compute windows first, then filter in a subsequent CTE so you can inspect all rows
notes: Current 30-day window:  ROWS BETWEEN 29 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
Prior (non-overlapping) 30-day window: ROWS BETWEEN 59 PRECEDING AND 30 PRECEDING

To drill into which category drove a drop: replicate the same two windows PARTITION BY category, then ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY anomaly_date ORDER BY drop DESC) to pick the worst category per anomaly day.
Percentage change: (current - prior) / NULLIF(prior, 0) * 100. Negative = decline, positive = growth.

title: LTV percentile tiers with cross-tier ratio benchmarking per segment
template:
WITH customer_ltv AS (
    SELECT c.customer_unique_id, c.customer_state,
        ROUND(SUM(oi.price + oi.freight_value), 2) AS ltv
    FROM customers c
    JOIN orders o       ON c.customer_id  = o.customer_id
    JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id     = oi.order_id
    WHERE o.order_status = 'delivered'
    GROUP BY c.customer_unique_id, c.customer_state
),
customer_tiers AS (
    SELECT customer_unique_id, customer_state, ltv,
        CASE
            WHEN PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ltv DESC) <= 0.05 THEN 'Top 5%'
            WHEN PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ltv DESC) <= 0.20 THEN '5-20%'
            WHEN PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ltv DESC) <= 0.50 THEN '20-50%'
            ELSE 'Bottom 50%'
        END AS tier
    FROM customer_ltv
),
state_tier_stats AS (
    SELECT customer_state, tier,
        COUNT(*)           AS customer_count,
        ROUND(AVG(ltv), 2) AS avg_ltv
    FROM customer_tiers
    GROUP BY customer_state, tier
),
state_pivot AS (
    SELECT customer_state,
        MAX(CASE WHEN tier = 'Top 5%' THEN avg_ltv END) AS top5_avg,
        MAX(CASE WHEN tier = '5-20%'  THEN avg_ltv END) AS mid_avg
    FROM state_tier_stats
    GROUP BY customer_state
)
SELECT sts.customer_state, sts.tier, sts.customer_count, sts.avg_ltv,
    ROUND(sp.top5_avg / NULLIF(sp.mid_avg, 0), 2) AS top5_to_mid_ratio,
    CASE WHEN sp.top5_avg < sp.mid_avg * 2 THEN 'FLAT DISTRIBUTION' ELSE '' END AS flag
FROM state_tier_stats sts
JOIN state_pivot sp ON sts.customer_state = sp.customer_state
ORDER BY sts.customer_state, sts.tier
when_to_use: assign customers to LTV percentile tiers (Top 5%, 5-20%, 20-50%, Bottom 50%) using PERCENT_RANK
compare tier averages within each geographic or demographic segment
flag segments where the top tier does not dominate (top5 avg < 2× mid-tier avg = flat distribution)
any analysis requiring: compute metric → assign tier → re-aggregate by (segment × tier) → compare tiers
anti_patterns: PERCENT_RANK ORDER BY ltv DESC means 0 = highest LTV customer; <= 0.05 captures the top 5% — if you use ASC, the logic inverts
computing PERCENT_RANK multiple times in one SELECT for different tiers is valid SQL but wasteful — wrap in a subquery and reference the computed rank column once
using NTILE(20) for 'Top 5%' — NTILE gives equal counts; PERCENT_RANK is correct for percentile-position tiers based on value rank
cross-tier pivot using SUM instead of MAX — there is only one avg_ltv per (state, tier) row; MAX(CASE WHEN tier='Top 5%' THEN avg_ltv END) collapses correctly
notes: Pivot technique: MAX(CASE WHEN tier = 'X' THEN avg_ltv END) in a GROUP BY state — this brings multiple tier rows for the same state onto one row for ratio computation.
Flat distribution flag: top5_avg < 2 × mid_avg indicates the top-tier customers are not dramatically more valuable than mid-tier — may signal pricing or retention opportunity.
Use NULLIF in the ratio denominator to prevent division by zero when a state has no mid-tier customers.

title: Multi-cause attribution with route-level baseline (seller vs carrier delay)
template:
WITH delivered AS (
    SELECT o.order_id, s.seller_state, c.customer_state,
        DATEDIFF(o.order_delivered_customer_date, o.order_estimated_delivery_date) AS delay_days,
        DATEDIFF(o.order_delivered_carrier_date,  o.order_purchase_timestamp)      AS handling_days,
        DATEDIFF(o.order_delivered_customer_date, o.order_delivered_carrier_date)  AS transit_days
    FROM orders o
    JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id    = oi.order_id
    JOIN customers c    ON o.customer_id  = c.customer_id
    JOIN sellers s      ON oi.seller_id   = s.seller_id
    WHERE o.order_status = 'delivered'
      AND o.order_delivered_customer_date > o.order_estimated_delivery_date
),
route_baseline AS (
    SELECT seller_state, customer_state,
        AVG(transit_days) AS median_transit  -- replace AVG with PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) if supported
    FROM delivered
    GROUP BY seller_state, customer_state
),
classified AS (
    SELECT d.order_id,
        CASE
            WHEN d.handling_days > 3 AND d.transit_days <= rb.median_transit * 1.2 THEN 'seller'
            WHEN d.handling_days <= 3 AND d.transit_days >  rb.median_transit * 1.2 THEN 'carrier'
            WHEN d.handling_days > 3 AND d.transit_days >  rb.median_transit * 1.2 THEN 'both'
            ELSE 'ambiguous'
        END AS delay_cause
    FROM delivered d
    JOIN route_baseline rb ON d.seller_state = rb.seller_state AND d.customer_state = rb.customer_state
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_delayed,
    ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN delay_cause = 'seller'    THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 1) AS pct_seller,
    ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN delay_cause = 'carrier'   THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 1) AS pct_carrier,
    ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN delay_cause = 'both'      THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 1) AS pct_both,
    ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN delay_cause = 'ambiguous' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 1) AS pct_ambiguous
FROM classified
when_to_use: attribute a delay (or any outcome) to one of multiple possible causes using independent thresholds
compare each entity's metric against a dynamic route/segment-level baseline (not a global threshold)
three-way or four-way classification: seller only / carrier only / both / neither
summary percentage rollup after individual row classification (SUM(CASE WHEN))
anti_patterns: using a single global baseline for transit time — routes from SP→SP take days; routes from SP→AM take weeks; always group by (origin_state, dest_state) or equivalent route key
classifying on the raw transit days without a route baseline — a 10-day transit is normal for long routes but alarming for local delivery
using PERCENTILE_CONT when it is not supported — fall back to AVG for the route median or compute it via NTILE
the 20% margin (transit_days <= median * 1.2) absorbs natural variance; a strict equal-to-median filter misclassifies many normal deliveries as carrier delays
notes: Compute all DATEDIFF columns inline in the first CTE to avoid repeating the expressions.

Route baseline (seller_state, customer_state) is computed from the same delayed orders set — this is a self-referencing baseline. For production, compute it on ALL delivered orders including on-time ones.
SUM(CASE WHEN delay_cause = 'X' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*) is the standard percentage rollup pattern — always divide by COUNT(*) of the full classified set.

title: Cross-sell / co-purchase pair analysis with canonical self-join deduplication
template:
WITH order_entities AS (
    -- One row per (order_id, entity) — deduplicate to avoid item-level explosion
    SELECT DISTINCT oi.order_id, COALESCE(t.category_english, p.category_name) AS entity
    FROM order_items oi
    JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
    LEFT JOIN category_translation t ON p.category_name = t.category_name
    WHERE p.category_name IS NOT NULL
),
order_totals AS (
    SELECT order_id, SUM(price + freight_value) AS order_value
    FROM order_items
    GROUP BY order_id
),
pairs AS (
    -- a.entity < b.entity ensures each unordered pair appears exactly once
    SELECT a.entity AS entity_a, b.entity AS entity_b, a.order_id
    FROM order_entities a
    JOIN order_entities b
        ON a.order_id = b.order_id
       AND a.entity < b.entity
)
SELECT p.entity_a, p.entity_b,
    COUNT(*)                       AS co_purchase_count,
    ROUND(AVG(ot.order_value), 2)  AS avg_basket_value
FROM pairs p
JOIN order_totals ot ON p.order_id = ot.order_id
GROUP BY p.entity_a, p.entity_b
ORDER BY co_purchase_count DESC
LIMIT 20
when_to_use: find which product categories (or products) are most frequently purchased together in the same order
market basket analysis, 'customers who bought X also bought Y'
any self-join where you want unordered pairs — always use a.key < b.key to avoid (A,B) and (B,A) appearing as separate rows
compute average basket value for orders containing a given pair
anti_patterns: self-joining on order_items directly without first reducing to (order_id, entity) — one order with 5 items of category A and 3 items of category B produces 15 pair rows instead of 1; always DISTINCT-reduce first
using a.entity != b.entity instead of a.entity < b.entity — this keeps both (A,B) and (B,A), doubling every count
joining on product_id for category analysis — categories with many products inflate counts; deduplicate to category level first
not using COALESCE for translated category names — untranslated rows return NULL and are excluded from pairs
notes: Canonical deduplication trick: a.key < b.key on the self-join ensures each unordered pair (A, B) where A < B appears exactly once.
Reduce to (order_id, category) DISTINCT before the self-join — this is the most important performance step; skipping it causes N² row explosion on large item tables.
Lift metric: co_purchase_count / (total_orders_with_A * total_orders_with_B / total_orders) — higher than 1 = true affinity. Not included above to keep template readable.

title: Sliding date-range window via self-join BETWEEN (sparse time-series)
template:
WITH entity_events AS (
    SELECT entity_id, DATE(event_timestamp) AS event_date, score
    FROM events
    JOIN related ON events.id = related.id
),
entity_baseline AS (
    SELECT entity_id,
        COUNT(*) AS total_events,
        SUM(CASE WHEN score = 5 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) * 1.0 / COUNT(*) AS baseline_rate
    FROM entity_events
    GROUP BY entity_id
),
window_seeds AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT entity_id, event_date AS window_start
    FROM entity_events
),
window_stats AS (
    SELECT ws.entity_id, ws.window_start,
        COUNT(ee.score) AS window_count,
        SUM(CASE WHEN ee.score = 5 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS window_5star,
        SUM(CASE WHEN ee.score = 5 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) * 1.0
            / NULLIF(COUNT(ee.score), 0) AS window_rate
    FROM window_seeds ws
    JOIN entity_events ee
        ON ws.entity_id = ee.entity_id
       AND ee.event_date BETWEEN ws.window_start AND ws.window_start + INTERVAL 6 DAY
    GROUP BY ws.entity_id, ws.window_start
)
SELECT ws.entity_id, ws.window_start,
    ws.window_5star AS spike_count,
    ROUND(ws.window_rate, 3) AS window_rate,
    ROUND(eb.baseline_rate, 3) AS baseline_rate,
    ROUND(ws.window_rate / NULLIF(eb.baseline_rate, 0), 2) AS spike_ratio
FROM window_stats ws
JOIN entity_baseline eb ON ws.entity_id = eb.entity_id
WHERE ws.window_5star >= 5
  AND ws.window_rate > eb.baseline_rate * 3
ORDER BY spike_ratio DESC
when_to_use: count events within a sliding date range when rows are sparse and ROWS BETWEEN gives wrong counts (ROWS BETWEEN counts rows, not calendar days)
detect temporal clustering: N+ events in any 7-day window per entity
compare a windowed rate to an entity-level baseline to find spikes
review bombing, fraud bursts, activity spikes — any 'X events in Y consecutive days' pattern
anti_patterns: using ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for a 7-day calendar window — ROWS counts rows, not days; if an entity has 2 events on day 1 and 1 on day 8, the 7-row window may span more than 7 calendar days
using every row as a window seed — only use DISTINCT (entity, date) as seeds; duplicate event dates on the same day seed redundant windows with identical counts
baseline rate from the same windowed data — always compute baseline from the full entity history, not just the anomaly window
forgetting NULLIF in spike_ratio denominator — an entity with baseline_rate = 0 (new entity) causes division by zero
notes: The BETWEEN join approach: for each (entity, seed_date), join all events WHERE event_date BETWEEN seed_date AND seed_date + INTERVAL 6 DAY. This is a range join — index on (entity_id, event_date) is critical for performance.
Spike ratio = window_rate / baseline_rate. A ratio > 3 means the windowed rate is 3× the entity's historical norm.
For Exasol / Postgres use + INTERVAL '6 days'; for MySQL use DATE_ADD(seed_date, INTERVAL 6 DAY).

title: Haversine great-circle distance in SQL with zip-code deduplication
template:
WITH geo_a AS (
    -- Average coordinates per zip code — geolocation tables often have duplicate rows per zip
    SELECT zip_code_prefix, AVG(lat) AS lat, AVG(lng) AS lng
    FROM geolocation
    GROUP BY zip_code_prefix
),
geo_b AS (
    SELECT zip_code_prefix, AVG(lat) AS lat, AVG(lng) AS lng
    FROM geolocation
    GROUP BY zip_code_prefix
),
order_distances AS (
    SELECT o.order_id,
        -- Haversine formula: distance in km
        6371 * 2 * ASIN(SQRT(
            POWER(SIN((RADIANS(gb.lat) - RADIANS(ga.lat)) / 2), 2)
            + COS(RADIANS(ga.lat)) * COS(RADIANS(gb.lat))
            * POWER(SIN((RADIANS(gb.lng) - RADIANS(ga.lng)) / 2), 2)
        )) AS distance_km,
        (oi.price + oi.freight_value) AS item_value,
        DATEDIFF(o.order_delivered_customer_date, o.order_estimated_delivery_date) AS delay_days,
        r.review_score
    FROM orders o
    JOIN order_items oi  ON o.order_id    = oi.order_id
    JOIN customers c     ON o.customer_id  = c.customer_id
    JOIN sellers s       ON oi.seller_id   = s.seller_id
    LEFT JOIN order_reviews r ON o.order_id = r.order_id
    JOIN geo_a ga ON s.seller_zip_code_prefix   = ga.zip_code_prefix
    JOIN geo_b gb ON c.customer_zip_code_prefix = gb.zip_code_prefix
    WHERE o.order_status = 'delivered'
),
banded AS (
    SELECT *,
        CASE
            WHEN distance_km < 100  THEN '1. <100km'
            WHEN distance_km < 500  THEN '2. 100-500km'
            WHEN distance_km < 1000 THEN '3. 500-1000km'
            ELSE                         '4. 1000km+'
        END AS distance_band
    FROM order_distances
)
SELECT distance_band,
    COUNT(*)                  AS order_count,
    ROUND(AVG(item_value), 2) AS avg_order_value,
    ROUND(AVG(review_score), 3) AS avg_review_score,
    ROUND(AVG(delay_days),   2) AS avg_delay_days,
    ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN delay_days > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 1) AS pct_late
FROM banded
WHERE review_score IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY distance_band
ORDER BY distance_band
when_to_use: compute straight-line (great-circle) distance between two geographic points given lat/lng
join seller and customer locations via zip code when direct lat/lng is not on the transaction table
bucket orders into distance bands (<100km, 100-500km, etc.) for delivery or pricing analysis
correlate distance with delivery delay, review score, freight cost, or conversion rate
anti_patterns: joining geolocation directly without deduplicating by zip first — geolocation tables typically have multiple rows per zip (different neighbourhoods); always GROUP BY zip and AVG(lat), AVG(lng) before joining
using INNER JOIN on zip code — some zip codes may not be in the geolocation table; use LEFT JOIN and handle NULLs or pre-filter to known zips
distance band labels must be sortable — prefix with '1. ', '2. ' etc. so ORDER BY distance_band sorts correctly as a string
applying Haversine in a WHERE clause without a CTE — the expression is expensive; compute it once in a CTE, then filter/aggregate downstream
notes: Haversine formula: 6371 * 2 * ASIN(SQRT(POWER(SIN((RADIANS(lat2) - RADIANS(lat1))/2),2) + COS(RADIANS(lat1)) * COS(RADIANS(lat2)) * POWER(SIN((RADIANS(lng2) - RADIANS(lng1))/2),2))). Earth radius = 6371 km.
Standard SQL trig functions: RADIANS(), SIN(), COS(), ASIN(), SQRT(), POWER() — available in Exasol, Postgres, MySQL, Snowflake, BigQuery.

For best-category-per-band: add a second CTE with GROUP BY (distance_band, category), then ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY distance_band ORDER BY AVG(review_score) DESC) to pick the top-rated category per band.
